# Appendix B. Divergence Reference {#sec-divergence .unnumbered}

The book teaches a portable baseline: commands and habits that behave
the same in the common cases across macOS, Linux, and WSL2 (which
behaves as Linux). This appendix is the other half of that promise, a
quick-reference list of where portability breaks. The recurring split
is macOS versus Linux: macOS ships the BSD versions of the core tools
and, by default, the zsh shell, while most Linux servers run the GNU
versions and bash. The values below are the typical observed defaults,
not guarantees; when it matters, check the version or setting on the
machine in front of you. Each row names the chapter that treats it in
full.

## Shell and platform basics {.unnumbered}

| Feature | macOS (BSD, zsh) vs Linux (GNU, bash) | Ch |
|---|---|---|
| Core tools and shell | macOS: BSD coreutils, zsh. Linux: GNU coreutils, bash | 1 |
| System bash version | macOS system bash frozen at `3.2.57` (2007, GPLv3); Linux bash 4+, `5.x` typical | 3, 11 |
| bash 3.2 gaps | macOS `/bin/bash` lacks `declare -A`, `${var^^}`, `mapfile`; present in bash 4+ | 11 |
| Install command | macOS `brew install X`; Linux `apt` / `dnf` / `pacman` | 3 |
| Checksum tool | macOS `shasum -a 256`; Linux `sha256sum`; the digest is identical | 3 |
| `which` | zsh builtin that knows aliases vs external `/usr/bin/which` | 2 |
| `--help` | BSD tools mostly answer with an error; GNU prints a usage summary | 2 |

Portable habit: target `#!/usr/bin/env bash` and install a modern bash
with `brew install bash`; prefer `command -v` over `which`; on a Mac
reach for `man`, not `--help`.

## Coreutils flags {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 5](chapters/05-navigation.qmd),
[Chapter 8](chapters/08-text-tabular-data.qmd), and
[Chapter 9](chapters/09-finding-things.qmd).

| Command | macOS (BSD) vs Linux (GNU) | Ch |
|---|---|---|
| `ls -l` `total` | BSD counts 512-byte blocks; GNU counts 1-KB blocks (not a file count) | 5 |
| `ls -G` | BSD turns color on and keeps the group; GNU suppresses the group column | 5 |
| `stat` | BSD `-f '%z %N %Sm'`; GNU `-c '%s %n %y'` | 5 |
| `readlink -f` / `realpath` | recent macOS has both; older macOS or bare BSD may lack them | 5 |
| `sed -i` | BSD requires a suffix (`-i.bak`); GNU makes it optional | 8 |
| `awk` | macOS ships BWK one-true-awk; Linux ships `gawk` with extensions | 8 |
| `find` regex | BSD `-E` before the path; GNU `-regextype posix-extended` | 9 |
| `find -printf` | absent on BSD (error); present on GNU | 9 |
| bare `find` | BSD needs a start path; GNU defaults to `.` | 9 |
| `ps` listing | `ps aux` works on both; `ps -ef` is the System V (Linux) idiom | 10 |
| `date` from epoch | BSD `date -r 1700000000`; GNU `date -d @1700000000` | 11 |
| `cat` show-all | BSD `cat -et` (no `-A`); GNU `cat -A` or `-et` | 12 |
| `/usr/bin/time` | BSD long report is `-l`; GNU is `-v` | 11 |

Portable habit: prefer the flags both share; write `sed -i.bak` (or
redirect to a new file); give `find` an explicit path and stick to
`-name`, `-type`, `-size`; use `ps aux`; use `wc -c` for a portable
byte count.

## Shell behavior {.unnumbered}

These are shell divergences (zsh vs bash), not coreutil ones. See
[Chapter 7](chapters/07-pipes-redirection-danger.qmd),
[Chapter 10](chapters/10-processes-permissions-secrets.qmd), and
[Chapter 18](chapters/18-startup-files-path.qmd).

| Behavior | zsh (macOS) vs bash (Linux) | Ch |
|---|---|---|
| Unmatched glob | zsh errors and aborts the command; bash passes the literal pattern through | 7 |
| Ignore-space history | zsh `setopt HIST_IGNORE_SPACE`; bash `HISTCONTROL=ignorespace`; off by default either way | 10 |
| Terminal tab type | Terminal.app and WezTerm open a login+interactive shell (read the profile and rc); many Linux emulators open a non-login interactive shell (read only the rc) | 18 |

Portable habit: never rely on a glob default (quote a literal
pattern); set your shell's ignore-space option in its rc; test a tab
with `[[ -o login ]]` (zsh) or `shopt -q login_shell` (bash); and in
scripts write `> file 2>&1`, not `&>`, since `&>` is undefined in
POSIX `sh` (dash on Debian and Ubuntu).

## Build and environment tools {.unnumbered}

| Tool | macOS vs Linux | Ch |
|---|---|---|
| GNU Make | macOS ships `3.81` (1-second mtime ties); Linux runs `4.x` (sub-second) | 12 |
| `rsync` | macOS ships `openrsync` (protocol 29); Linux ships rsync `3.x` (protocol 31) | 14 |
| `uv.lock` | universal on both; the restored environment is platform-built either way | 13 |

Portable habit: `brew install make` for a modern `gmake`; stick to the
common `rsync` flags and run `rsync --version` on a new machine; commit
the lockfile and git-ignore the built environment.

## The PowerShell sidebar {.unnumbered}

Windows researchers are steered to WSL2, so PowerShell gets awareness
only. It is not a second Unix shell; four things bite anyone who
follows Unix instructions inside it. See
[Chapter 3](chapters/03-setup.qmd).

- **The pipeline carries objects, not text.** PowerShell `|` passes
  structured .NET objects between cmdlets, so the text-filtering model
  (`grep`, `cut`, `sed`) does not transfer.
- **The aliases are fake friends.** `ls` and `cat` are aliases for
  `Get-ChildItem` / `Get-Content`: the names work, the options do not.
  Windows PowerShell 5.1 also aliased `curl` / `wget` to
  `Invoke-WebRequest`; PowerShell 7 removed those two.
- **Execution policy blocks scripts.** Windows clients refuse to run
  scripts by default until you set an execution policy, and even a
  permissive setting like `RemoteSigned` still blocks unsigned
  downloaded scripts.
- **Paths and separators differ.** Windows uses backslashes and a `;`
  PATH separator (`C:\...`, `;`); Unix uses forward slashes and `:`.

See the [command cheat sheet](#sec-cheatsheet) for the portable form
of each command, and the [panic reference](#sec-panic) for where a
divergence changes a command's destructive behavior.
